SDU

ReceiptIQ

A Mobile Application for Personal Finance Management
Supported by Optical Character Recognition & Artificial Intelligence
Burak SAYAN
Supervisor: Prof. Dr. Ecir Uğur KÜÇÜKSİLLE
SDU
1992
INTRODUCTION

Personal expense tracking largely relies on manual data entry, which is time-consuming and error-prone. Digitizing paper receipts and automatically categorizing financial data would enable users to manage their budgets more effectively.

In this study, a mobile personal finance management application named ReceiptIQ was developed. It integrates OCR technology for extracting text from Turkish receipts, a hybrid machine learning system for spending categorization, and a RAG-based AI assistant for natural-language financial queries.

MOTIVATION & PURPOSE
  • Most personal finance apps in Turkey require manual input, reducing consistent usage and accuracy.
  • OCR-based automatic scanning eliminates data entry effort and significantly improves user experience.
  • Automatic classification across 15 spending categories enables analysis of user spending habits.
  • Budget limit monitoring and anomaly detection notify users in real time when unusual spending occurs.
  • An AI-powered chat assistant allows users to query their financial data using natural language.
  • Savings goals, recurring expense tracking, and weekly summaries support long-term financial planning.
MATERIAL & METHODS
  • Mobile: React Native + Expo (iOS & Android)
  • Backend: Python, FastAPI, PostgreSQL, Railway Cloud
  • OCR: Google Cloud Vision API (primary) + Tesseract OCR (fallback)
  • Classification: Keyword rule engine + TF-IDF & Logistic Regression hybrid model
  • Vector DB: Qdrant — powers the RAG-based AI assistant
  • Notifications: Expo Push Notification API + APScheduler cron jobs
  • Auth: JWT-based token authentication
  • Deployment: Docker containers on Railway platform
Receipt Scanning
OCR Review
Dashboard
RESULTS

Google Cloud Vision API achieved a 79–83% confidence score extracting 27–58 lines of text on real Turkish receipts — significantly outperforming Tesseract OCR, which frequently returned zero values for amounts and misread Turkish characters.

Budget Management
Anomaly Alert
AI Assistant
  • The hybrid classifier correctly categorized Starbucks receipts as Food, Shell stations as Fuel, and BİM markets as Grocery, resolving the pure-ML overfitting problem.
  • Statistical anomaly detection (z-score > 2.0, min. 5 prior records) successfully flagged unusual spending with push + email notifications.
  • The RAG-based AI assistant answered user queries such as "How much did I spend last month?" using personalized receipt context from Qdrant.
  • All planned modules — savings goals, recurring expenses, weekly summaries, tag system, CSV/PDF export — were fully implemented and tested.
ARGUMENT

All project goals were successfully achieved. OCR-based data extraction, hybrid classification, anomaly detection, budget management, and the AI assistant were fully integrated. The key challenge — Tesseract's poor performance on Turkish text — was resolved by adopting Google Cloud Vision as the primary OCR provider. The ML model's overfitting on synthetic data was overcome with the keyword rule engine. Future work includes retraining the model with real user feedback data and adding bank API integration for automatic transaction import.

ACKNOWLEDGEMENTS

The author would like to thank Prof. Dr. Ecir Uğur KÜÇÜKSİLLE for his valuable guidance and supervision throughout this project, and to his family for their unconditional support during the entire academic journey.
